observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to conduct immediate reconciliation of the individual balances of the Cash-in Bank account with the pertinent Cashbooks and the Cash-in Bank accounts and to make the necessary adjustments thereon.
Further, direct the Municipal Accountant to record the reconciling items in the books of accounts based on the Bank Reconciliation Statements (BRS) prepared. Moreover, for the Municipal Accountant to also furnish the Municipal Treasurer copies of the BRS for the recording of reconciling items, if any, pertaining to her records.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to review and take appropriate steps to collect the dormant receivables of the LGU. Thereafter, request for authority to write-off all accounts deemed uncollectible, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.
Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant to conduct regular monitoring and analysis of receivables to ensure that these are collected timely when the accounts become due and demandable.

We recommended that the Management direct the Municipal Accountant to:
Timely prepare the report of liquidation of the funds and to maintain and update the records or Subsidiary Ledgers pertaining to the fund transfers to facilitate the verification of the outstanding balance and to initiate the return of any unused balance of the fund transfer to the SAs, after verification of the status of the funds and in case of the completion of the project, pursuant to the afore-cited regulations.
Classify funds which are considered as special purpose funds so that they could be retained and identify fund transfers whose nature are financial assistance/subsidy and fund transfers that does indicate disposition of the unused balances so that they can be reverted to the unappropriated surplus of the Municipality for future utilization thereof subject to the usual accounting, auditing and budgeting laws and regulations.
Conduct periodic monitoring, analysis and reconciliation of the funds received from other agencies and take note of non-moving accounts and unidentified balances, as well as those whose nature could not be determined and undertake appropriate actions, as well as effect the necessary adjustments on the deficiencies to fairly present the balances of the Due to other NGAs account.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management:
Instruct the Property Officer, to prepare, in coordination with the Municipal Engineering Office, and submit the Property Insurance Form to the General Insurance Fund, GSIS, copy furnished the Auditor, in compliance with COA Circular 2018-002. Ensure that all the insurable properties and assets of the Municipality are included in the said listing;
Provide adequate funds in the succeeding Annual Budget to cover the Insurance Expenses for all insurable properties of the LGU and secure insurance coverage for all insurable properties; and
Ensure that the insurance coverage of the insurable properties and assets of the Municipality are promptly renewed every year and that adequate insurance coverage are secured to provide protection and indemnification for any damage to or loss the assets due to fire, earthquake, storm or other calamities.

We recommended that the Municipality adhere to the mandatory posting on the GPPB Online Portal of information relative to Emergency Procurements under the Bayanihan Act, to ensure that accountability and transparency in the procurement is upheld.

We recommended that Management require concerned officials to submit copies of Contracts/POs to the Auditor, with the required supporting documents attached thereto, within five days after perfection thereof, and the related Notification of Delivery of Services/Supplies and Materials, within 24 hours after acceptance of such deliveries of services/supplies and materials, in compliance with the requirements of COA Circular No. 2009-001 and COA Circular 96-010.

We reiterated our recommendation to the Management to:
Furnish the Audit Team, at the beginning of the year, a list of on-going and programs, projects and activities that will be implemented during the year.
Submit to the Audit Team the Monthly Monitoring Report on the Status of Programs, Projects and Activities within ten days after the end of each month.

We recommended that Management to require the Property/Supply Officer Designate to monitor and comply strictly with the reglementary period on the submission of Contracts involving government spaces, market/slaughterhouse stalls leased to government/private individuals pursuant to the above regulation.

We recommended Management:
a. Instruct all concerned Municipal Collecting officers to strictly observe proper collection procedures pursuant to the Local Treasury Manual Book V; and
b. Require the concerned personnel to furnish the Auditor or give access to copies of the RPT Bill and Assessment Forms to facilitate determination of the correctness and completeness of the reported collections.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all cash advances for payment of wages of job orders for the calendar year 2021 and henceforth, be duly supported with Approved Job Order Contracts (every new contract and/or renewal of contract) and Daily Time Records.
Further, we recommended that Management direct the Disbursing Officer to ensure that all liquidation of cash advances for payment of wages of job orders for calendar year 2021 and henceforth, be duly supported with duly verified/accepted accomplishment report, pursuant to specific sections of PD 1445 and COA Circular 2012-001.

In view thereof, we recommended that:
The VM and Members of the SB stop the practice of granting assistance to the constituent of the Municipality, charged against the respective funds as this is not among their mandated duties and functions as per laws and rules and regulations specified above,
Management should adhere to the applicable laws, rules and regulations particularly Section 2 and 4 (1) of PD 1445, and
Properly classify expenses according to their respective account name, and description to properly present such transactions in the financial statement.

We recommended that Management:
Direct the Municipal Accountant to cause the immediate submission of the lacking documents enumerated in Annex A hereof, otherwise, the non-submission of the required supporting documents shall warrant the issuance of Notice of Suspension/Disallowance in audit; and
Henceforth, enjoin the Municipal to process and pay only those disbursement vouchers supported with complete documentations in full compliance with Section 4, Paragraph 6 of P.D. No. 1445 and Section 9.1.3.1 of COA Circular 2012-001.

We recommended that the LCE provide justification why the mandatory requirement for a CPES is not observed.  Henceforth, we enjoin LCE to revisit the provisions as provided under Section 13 of the Annex “E” of the R.A. No. 9184 and its IRR on the evaluation of contractor’s performance.  Further, that concerned department representatives be convened to discuss on the subject matter to ascertain commitment/s in terms of agency resources towards conformance to the mandatory requirement for a CPES and its necessary impact to the operations and structure of the LGU.

We recommended that Management must ensure the following:
That the surety bond issued by contractors as performance bonds on infrastructure projects shall contain the specific dates of coverage, specifically the date starting from the effectivity of the bond up to the time of completion of the project and up to one year from completion, including the expiration date thereof. Henceforth, surety bonds posted by contractors with inconsistent or unclear stipulations relative to the validity period are not accepted as Performance Bond.
That projects with approved contract time extensions are supported with evidence that the validity of the performance security is correspondingly extended pursuant to Section 11.5 of Annex “E” of the IRR of RA 9184.

We recommended that management require the contractors to have the materials tested by laboratories duly accredited by DOST/BRS and submit the results thereof to ensure the quality of the materials to be used for infrastructure projects, and the Municipal Engineer to ensure compliance thereof.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management, through the GAD Focal Point System (GFPS) facilitate the full establishment of the GAD database in order to have a basis for planning and programming PPAs and formulating policies that are gender-responsive and to be able to undertake thorough gender analysis based on relevant data to properly identify existing gender issues and ensure that the approved PPAs to be incorporated in the GAD Plan and Budget are gender responsive.
We recommended that Management establish a Monitoring and Evaluation System to enable it to assess the effectiveness and efficiency of its gender mainstreaming efforts.

We recommended that the GAD Focal Person observe the timely submission of GAD Accomplishment Report and ensure that it properly reflects GAD Budget, Funds, and corresponding utilization of identified projects.

We recommended to the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management (LDRRM) Officer to optimize the utilization of the LDRRMF of the LGU in order to strengthen its disaster preparedness and avoid potential damages during disasters.
Likewise, to address the gaps in DRRM planning, implementation and monitoring of the programs, projects and activities, we recommended close coordination among LGU officials and other DRRM officer to ensure effective formulation and implementation of the DRR Projects as listed in the LDRRMFIP.
We recommended that the Local DRRM Office be institutionalized and organized according to law by assigning full-time permanent employees thereat to maximize its efficiency and effectiveness.

We recommended that the municipality strive to improve its efficiency on the utilization of the 20 per cent EDF and to fully implement the priority PPAs, in order to attain the desired outcomes on the socio-economic development that will benefit the intended beneficiaries.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Engineer and Municipal Planning and Development Officer to hasten, monitor and prioritize the implementation and completion of the remaining Social and Economic Development Projects under the 20 per cent EDF.

We reiterated our recommendation that Management prepare and submit the reports and outputs enumerated in DILG MC No. 2012-120 by the end of current year, copy furnished the Audit Team, for the proper and effective implementation of LCPC programs, projects and activities

We recommended that the LCE require the Municipal Accountant to review and verify the dormant receivables of the LGU and issue demand letters to recipients/grantees.  If the accounts remain uncollected despite the issuance of at least two demand letters, request for authority to write off the doubtful accounts, in accordance with COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant shall conduct regular monitoring and analysis of receivables to ensure that these are timely collected when the accounts become due and demandable.

Further, direct the concerned Supply Officers to submit to the Municipal Accountant the corresponding utilization reports on the inventories in order that these may be dropped from the books.

Henceforth, we recommended that Management require the LSB to obtain from the DepEd ROV representatives the aforecited Program, Recommendation and approved SIP and DEDP prior to the preparation of the Annual SEF Budget, as guide/basis for the preparation and approval of the Annual SEF Budget by the LSB, and copy furnished the Audit Team.

We recommended that Management continue its improved performance in the submission of the monthly trial balances and the quarterly financial statements. Henceforth, strive to submit the required financial reports on or before the deadlines set.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC), in coordination with the Municipal Engineer, submit the required PMR to the GPPB on a semestral basis pursuant to Section 12.2 of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 and GPPB Circular 03-2015 dated March 20, 2015.

We recommended that Management require the General Services Officer to immediately submit the Monthly Reports of Fuel Consumption for CY 2020. Henceforth, submit the report within ten days after the end of the month to enable the Auditor to conduct prompt verification thereof.

We recommended that Management establish the Municipal Internal Audit Office following the guidelines set forth by DBM Local Budget Circular (LBC) No. 110 in the organizational structure and staffing of IAS/U to further improve and strengthen the internal control system of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive:
Direct the LDRRM Officer to properly organize the office and install the necessary systems and procedures pursuant to R.A. No. 10121;
Adhere to the full-time staffing requirements of the LDRRM Office; and
Require the LDRRM Officer and her staff to attend LDRRM trainings/read DRRM materials to equip them with the required knowledge to efficiently and effectively perform their assigned duties and responsibilities.

We recommended that Management require the LSB to formulate/prepare the required Three-Year Strategic Program to serve as reference in the preparation of the SEF budget.
Henceforth, the LSB strictly follow the required processes and documents in planning and budgeting for SEF under the revised guidelines on the use of SEF pursuant to DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular (JC) No. 1, s. 2017.
We recommended that LSB strictly observe submission of the required reports to the concerned oversight department/agency to promote transparency and accountability on the SEF.

We recommended that Management prepare and submit the necessary reports enumerated in item No. 1 of Part II of the above-quoted DILG MC by the end of current year for the proper and effective implementation of PPAs in the succeeding year.

We recommended that Management ensure that programs, projects and activities identified in the Work and Financial Plan of the LCPC be implemented in order to deliver the vital services to the children of the Municipality.

In keeping with GPPB Resolution No.09-2009, we recommended that the LCE:

Instruct the end-users to set precise and clear parameters reflective of all its requirements for the goods that it wishes to acquire to be able to procure the right products that will serve its needs efficiently and effectively;

Direct the BAC and the Technical Working Group (TWG) to clarify/validate information contained in the procurement documents submitted by the supplier/bidder to ensure that the Purchase Order (PO)/Contract is awarded to the most responsive and complying bidder; and

Require MGSO to ensure complete information/ specifications in the Inspection and Acceptance Report of the actual deliveries received as contained in the Delivery Receipt and verified through actual inspection.

We recommended that Management:

Instruct the Municipal Engineer, Municipal Planning and Development Officer, and the BAC to identify the specific location of a particular project to be implemented by the Municipality in the pertinent documents; and

Require the Municipal Engineer to ensure that complete information/details are indicated in the Report of Acceptance and Turn-over in relation to completed projects and in other reports pertaining to projects implemented by the Municipality.
We recommended that the LCE require the SWM Committee to comply with the instructions of the EMB and revise the SWM Plan to comply with requirements of the Ecological SWM Act of 2000.

We recommended that Management:

Instruct the Building Official or the ME to review and conduct inspection of firms/establishments that obtained a Building Permit during the year to determine whether the building is not being occupied or is not being utilized pending issuance of a corresponding Certificate of Occupancy and inform the owners of the firms/establishments in writing, about the result of their review and inspection and require them to apply for Certificate of Occupancy if warranted; and

Require the ME to prepare and submit monthly to the Municipal Assessor the List of Building Permittees and Certificate of Occupancy issued by their Office, in order for the Municipal Assessor to be able also to conduct inspection for the issuance of Tax Declaration on the Building/Structure issued with Certificate of Occupancy.
observations.reason: Lack of manpower

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Sangguniang Bayan review, revise and amend the existing Guinobatan Revised Tax Code of 2008 and request the LCE to require the concerned Department Heads to prepare a proposed adjustment on tax rates as prescribed under the Code and come up with one updated Revenue Code for the LGU; and

We also recommended for the LCE to direct the Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Assessor, and other concerned Department Heads to prepare and submit to the Sangguniang Bayan for approval the proposed adjustment on tax rates and come up with a consolidated updated revenue code in an effort to increase/maximize its revenue collections which will result in higher income for the LGU to augment the budget provision for a better delivery of government services.
observations.reason: The LGU is in the process of updating the Local Tax Code

Henceforth, strictly observe the timelines set for the rendition/submission of accounts to avoid suspensions/ disallowances in audit.

Trace back the beginning balances for the variousInter-Agency Payables accounts to gradually sanitize the accounting records; and

Maintain GLs in the manner prescribed by the NGAS Manual for LGUs.

Require the Municipal Engineer to prepare and submit monthly to the Municipal Assessor the List of Building Permittees and Certificate of Occupancy issued by their Office, in order for the Municipal Assessor to be able also to conduct inspection for the issuance of Tax Declaration on the Building/Structure issued with Certificate of Occupancy

We recommended that Management direct, the Municipal Planning and Development Office (MPDO) and Municipal Engineering Office to prioritize, optimally utilize and promptly implement Programs, Projects and Activities funded by the 20 per cent EDF in accordance with the timelines. Despite personnel limitations, these Offices have to strategize so that projects chargeable to the fund could be implemented within the most reasonable time to ensure socio-economic benefits projected to be enjoyed by its constituents within the budget year and ensure continuous economic development in the Municipality which are essential in the attainment of its goals and objectives in improving the quality of life of its constituents.
Also, to prioritize barangays who are ready to implement projects and regularly communicate/coordinate with the respective barangay officials on Municipal Aid Projects that will be undertaken in the Municipality to address issues in the implementation of projects in the barangays of LGU.

We recommended that the Sangguniang Bayan review, revise and amend the existing Guinobatan Revised Tax Code of 2008 and request the LCE to require the concerned Department Heads to prepare proposed adjustments on tax rates as prescribed under the Code and come up with one updated Revenue Code for the LGU.
We also recommended that the LCE direct the Municipal Treasurer, Municipal Assessor and other concerned Department Heads to prepare and submit to the Sangguniang Bayan for approval the proposed adjustment on tax rates and come up with a consolidated updated revenue code in an effort to increase/maximize its revenue collections which will result in higher income for the LGU to augment the budget provision for a better delivery of government services.

DILG-ROV as per BUB/GPBP Guidelines. Also, according to the Management, aside
from the CSOs, the Grantor-Agencies who monitor the progress of the project
implementation provide for a list of reports and other requirements to be accomplished by
the focal persons which will be then submitted to them. In addition, the Grantor-Agencies
and the CSOs monitor the project even after its completion to see if it is being properly
maintained.

Likewise, not 2ll BUB/GPB Projects have been fully implemented as of March 7,
2017. Office Memoranda was also issued by the Management to the concerned personnel
in order to rectify the lapses in complying with the contractual provisions of the MOA.

‘We recommended that Management:

a. Evaluate and determine the causes why the identified PPAs under the
LDRRMF of the Municipality were not fully implemented during the year,
despite these being incorporated in the LDRRMP and LDRRMIP; and

b. Henceforth, adopt and formulate a sound and implementable LDRRMP
and LDRRMIP, pursuant to the above-mentioned law, rules and
regulations, by carefully identifying the most important and priority PPAs
and exert effort to maximize its use vis-a-vis minimize disaster risks and
problems.

Management pointed out that there were some factors that contributed to the huge
unexpended balance of the LDRRMF and also the low implementation rate of PPAs. One
of those is the low frequency of typhoons/calamities that affected the Municipality in
CY 2016 which resulted to the huge unexpended amount appropriated under the Quick
Response Fund. Likewise, this has brought down purchases such as Food Supplies under

the 70% Disaster Prevention, Mitigation and Preparedness Program Fund.

Under the Capital outlay, rehabilitation projects were delayed as they have waited
for the NGAs, such as the DPWH, to implement their rehabilitation projects of the same
nature in the same location to avoid duplication.

Management gave an assurance that they will adhere to Section 305(g) and (h) of
R.A. No. 7160 and will undertake measures such as updating their LDRRMP to make it
sound, implementable and responsive to LDRRM concerns.

‘We recommended that Management require the LSB of the Municipality:

a. To properly identify and categorize programs, projects and activities that
are to be funded out of the SEF in the Annual Budget of the SEF and
include in the Annual Procurement Plan of the Municipality, so that only
those PPAs that can be implemented during the year are included and to
facilitate its implementation; and

b. To plan thoroughly the Budget for the SEF to maximize the
implementation of priority PPAs, to ensure that optimum benefits are
derived by the greater number of its students in the public elementary and
secondary schools within the locality.

The audit observation was communicated by the Management with the Local
School Board and the Local Chief Executive and has required the School Supervisor to
closely monitor the implementation and utilization of Special Education Fund extended
under their jurisdiction and ensured that timetables are met.

‘We recommended that the Management:

a. Advise the Members of the Local School Board, that in the preparation of
School Board Budget, to give priority to programs, activities and projects
enumerated in Section 272 of the Local Government Code and Joint
Circular No. 01, s. of 1998 of the DECS-DBM-DILG and therefore mast
utilize the proceeds of the SEF only for such purposes prescribed by law,
rules and regulations; and

b. Charge the expenses incurred for the payment of the above-cited computer
printer to be used in the District and Principal Offices and other non-SEF
expenses, against the funds of the Department of Education or the General
Fund of the LGU, otherwise this warrant the issuance of Notice of
Disallowance by the Audit Team.

The Local Chief Executive has issued a memorandum endorsing the said AOM to
the Local School Board with the advice to only give priority to programs, projects and
activities enumerated in Section 272 of the Local Government Code and Joint Circular No.
01 of 1998 of the DECS-DBM-DILG.

According to the School Supervisors present during the exit conference, the unit
will be transferred to the IT Section of the School to be used by the students.

‘We recommended that Management see to it that every annual GAD Plan and
Budget be duly approved/endorsed by oversight agencies especially by the PCW in
order to ensure that identified PPAs were authorized by laws on gender and
development. Also, an Annual GAD Accomplishment Report should always be
prepared and submitted to COA on or before the deadline pursuant to COA Circular
No. 2014-001.

According to the Management, they were not aware that it must be approved by
PCW nor seminars and trainings regarding GAD stated that Gad Plan must be approved in
the said Commission. They will seek guidance/assistance on the matter with the provincial
government if this should be the case.

We recommended to the GAD Focal Person to strictly monitor the
implementation of GAD PPAs by the concerned Offices in order that benefits due the
targeted beneficiaries are duly provided and ensure full and complete GAD
accomplishment every year.

Management will hire additional personnel, preferably job order, to monitor GAD
Plans and Accomplishment.

‘We recommended to Management that:

a. Municipal Solid Waste Management Board review, update and cause the
approval by appropriate authorities of its 10- year Solid Waste Management
Plan, pursuant to Section 16 of Republic Act. No.9003, dated January 16,
2001;

b. Municipality adopt safety and preventive measures in order that its current
open dumpsite at Brgy. Ilawod will not provide the same result as that of its
former open dumpsite at Brgy. Dofia Mercedes. The Municipality has to
secure appropriate permits and authority for the alternative use of the said
open dumpsite;

c. Immediate conversion of the Municipality’s dumpsite to an Ecological Park or
Sanitary Landfill (SLF) be undertaken, in compliance with Section 37 of the
Act; and

d. Intensification of the SWM information dissemination campaigns, trainings
and seminars be undertaken and enforcement of all regulations relative to
Solid Waste Management be effected, to the extent of imposing penalties for
non-compliance.

Management pointed out that they have the 10 year Solid Waste Management Plan
but what is needed is a comprehensive one. They are in the process of making the 10 year
SWMP more comprehensive and they will seck the approval of the appropriate authorities.

‘With regard to the open dumpsite in Brgy. llawod, Management commented that it
was not really a dumpsite but rather a Material Recovery Facility in which all garbage are
being segregated and shredded for recycling purposes.

Also, Mapagement has incorporated in their plan proper trainings for personnel
involved in Solid Waste Management.

observations.recommendation: (50) We recommended that the local government unit establish or formulate specific guidelines in the granting of all kinds of financial assistance to its constituents to ensure an efficient implementation of the program and economical utilization of its limited resources. The guidelines may include, among others, the following: a). Qualifications of the recipients, to include income levels which may be determined by the screening committee for all kinds of financial assistance; b). For medical assistance-Categorize ailments, such as ordinary, medical or surgical; c). Maximum and reasonable amount of financial assistance for each category; d). Frequency or maximum number of entitlements in a year; e). Mode of payment; f). Uniform authorization and documentary requirement such as certificate from the barangay, income levels, social worker case study, official receipts, hospital bills and other basic requirements to establish validity and correctness of the claims.
observations.reason: Compounded cases involving illness, length of illness, medical requirements of illness, etc., must be looked into to formulate a more accurate categorization.
